topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating sound that seemed to amplify the tension in my chest. I stared at the application form, the ink blurring under my thumb. Liam, bless his oblivious heart, had applied for the same position. It felt like a personal slight, a deliberate act of sabotage. I clenched my jaw so tightly my molars ached. Every time I thought about his name next to mine on that list, a cold knot tightened in my stomach, making me want to physically recoil from the desk. I reread the job description, dissecting every requirement, searching for some minuscule flaw in his qualifications, anything to justify my own superior suitability. When I finally hit 'submit,' my hand trembled slightly, a tremor born not of excitement, but of simmering resentment.

[story 2]

"Seriously? He applied too?" I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible over the keyboard clacking. My knuckles were white where I gripped the mouse, the pressure building behind my eyes. It was infuriating. Liam, with his easy confidence and effortless charm, had managed to snag the same opportunity I’d been angling for. I leaned back in my chair, deliberately stretching my arms above my head, a slow, deliberate movement designed to showcase a simmering irritation. I watched him across the room, his easy smile completely unbothered. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I felt a sharp, acidic burn rising in my throat, a sensation that made me want to shout, to lash out, but I kept my voice level, a carefully constructed mask of polite indifference.

[story 3]

The air in the coffee shop was thick with the scent of burnt sugar and stale espresso, but all I could smell was sourness. I traced the lines on the paper where I’d written my personal statement, my pen moving with unnecessary violence. Liam had applied. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I picked up my lukewarm latte, the ceramic cup feeling heavy and cold in my hand. I took a long, slow sip, letting the bitterness of the coffee mirror the churning in my gut. My gaze drifted towards the entrance, and I saw him—casual, relaxed. A deep, internal churning began, a restless energy that made my legs feel unsteady beneath the table. I wanted to rearrange the furniture, to make the space feel hostile, to express the sheer unfairness of it all through some dramatic, silent gesture.
